01.05.03.05.001sa: What two categories of factors determine the mechanical properties of the dermis?

## Dermal Mechanical Properties
- The dermis gets its physical characteristics from the **extracellular matrix (ECM)**
- Two key factors:
  1. **Composition**: what the ECM is made of (collagen, elastin, ground substance)
  2. **Fiber arrangement**: how those fibers are organized and oriented
- Different regions of the body have different fiber arrangements, which is why skin behaves differently depending on location
01.05.03.05.002sa: What percentage of the dermis's dry weight is composed of collagen?

## Collagen Content of the Dermis
- Collagen makes up roughly **70-80%** of the dermis by dry weight
- The predominant type is **type I collagen**
- This heavy collagen concentration is what gives skin its tensile strength
- Tensile strength means resistance to stretching and tearing
01.05.03.05.003sa: What is the predominant type of collagen found in the dermis?

## Dermal Collagen
- **Type I collagen** is the dominant collagen in the dermis
- It is also the most abundant collagen type in the body overall
- Provides **tensile strength**, resisting stretching and tearing
- Makes up ~70-80% of the dermis by dry weight
01.05.03.05.004sa: What mechanical property does collagen provide to the dermis?

## Collagen and Tensile Strength
- Collagen provides **tensile strength**: the ability to resist stretching and tearing
- Primarily type I collagen, making up ~70-80% of dermal dry weight
- Works alongside elastic fibers, which handle stretch and recoil
- Together these two fiber types give skin both durability and flexibility
01.05.03.05.005sa: In the context of dermal collagen, what does tensile strength specifically resist?

## Tensile Strength in the Dermis
- **Tensile strength** is the ability to withstand pulling forces without breaking
- In the dermis, this means resisting both:
  1. **Stretching**: being pulled apart
  2. **Tearing**: being ripped
- Collagen (type I) is the primary source of this property
- When the dermis is stretched beyond its capacity, collagen fibers tear, producing stretch marks (striae)
01.05.03.05.006sa: What two structural components make up elastic fibers in the dermis?

## Elastic Fiber Composition
- Elastic fibers consist of two components:
  1. **Elastin**: the core protein that provides stretch and recoil
  2. **Fibrillin microfibrils**: glycoprotein scaffold that surrounds and supports the elastin
- Together they allow skin to stretch and snap back
- Age-related degradation of these fibers leads to wrinkles and sagging
01.05.03.05.007sa: What mechanical function do elastic fibers provide to the dermis?

## Elastic Fiber Function
- Elastic fibers allow the skin to **stretch and recoil** (snap back to original shape)
- Composed of elastin + fibrillin microfibrils
- Complement collagen, which provides tensile strength
- In young skin, collagen + elastin work together so skin can stretch and return to shape
- Aging degrades elastic fibers, reducing recoil and contributing to wrinkles and sagging
01.05.03.05.008sa: In young skin, what protein provides strength while another provides recoil?
Collagen provides strength; elastin provides recoil

## Collagen-Elastin Partnership
- **Collagen**: provides tensile strength (resists stretching/tearing)
- **Elastin**: provides recoil (snap-back after stretching)
- In young skin, both are abundant and functional
- Result: skin can stretch and return to its original shape
- Aging degrades both proteins, leading to wrinkles and sagging
01.05.03.05.009sa: What visible changes result from age-related degradation of collagen and elastin in the skin?

## Aging and Dermal Fibers
- With age, both collagen and elastin degrade
- **Loss of collagen**: reduced tensile strength
- **Loss of elastin**: reduced recoil
- Visible results:
  - **Wrinkles**: skin no longer snaps back smoothly
  - **Sagging**: skin loses structural support and droops
- Young skin has abundant, functional versions of both fibers
01.05.03.05.010sa: What are cleavage lines in the skin also called? (Name both alternate terms.)
One named for a person, one descriptive

## Cleavage Line Terminology
- Three interchangeable terms:
  1. **Cleavage lines**
  2. **Tension lines**
  3. **Langer lines** (named after Karl Langer, who mapped them in the 1860s)
- All refer to lines on the skin surface that reflect the predominant orientation of collagen bundles in the reticular dermis
01.05.03.05.011sa: What term describes the lines on the skin surface that reflect the predominant orientation of collagen bundles in the reticular dermis?

## Cleavage Lines
- Reflect the **dominant direction of collagen fiber bundles** in the reticular dermis
- Also called tension lines or Langer lines
- Orientation varies by body region:
  - **Limbs**: longitudinal
  - **Trunk/neck**: circumferential
- Surgically important: incisions parallel to these lines heal better with less scarring
01.05.03.05.012sa: In which layer of the dermis do the collagen fiber bundles that define cleavage lines reside?

## Cleavage Lines and the Reticular Dermis
- Cleavage lines are determined by collagen bundle orientation in the **reticular dermis**
- The reticular dermis is the deeper, thicker layer of the dermis
- Contains dense irregular connective tissue with thick collagen bundles
- The dominant direction of these bundles varies by body region and determines the direction of greatest mechanical stress
01.05.03.05.013sa: What determines the dominant direction of collagen fiber bundles in a given skin region?
The direction of greatest mechanical stress

## Collagen Orientation and Mechanical Stress
- Collagen fiber bundles align along the direction of **greatest mechanical stress** in each region
- This alignment produces the visible pattern known as cleavage lines (Langer lines)
- Orientation varies by location:
  - **Limbs**: longitudinal (along the long axis)
  - **Trunk and neck**: circumferential (wrapping around)
01.05.03.05.014sa: What is the orientation of cleavage lines on the limbs?

## Cleavage Line Orientation
- **Limbs**: cleavage lines run **longitudinally** (along the length of the limb)
- **Trunk and neck**: cleavage lines run **circumferentially** (wrapping around)
- This reflects the dominant direction of collagen bundles in the reticular dermis
- Surgeons use this information to plan incision direction for optimal healing
01.05.03.05.015sa: What is the orientation of cleavage lines on the trunk and neck?

## Cleavage Line Orientation
- **Trunk and neck**: cleavage lines run **circumferentially** (encircling the body)
- **Limbs**: cleavage lines run **longitudinally** (along the length)
- The pattern reflects collagen bundle orientation in the reticular dermis
- Collagen aligns along the direction of greatest mechanical stress in each region
01.05.03.05.016sa: Why do surgical incisions made parallel to cleavage lines heal better and produce less scarring?
The incision separates collagen bundles rather than cutting across them

## Cleavage Lines and Surgical Incisions
- Cleavage lines reflect dominant collagen bundle orientation in the reticular dermis
- **Parallel incision**: separates bundles along their natural alignment
  - Less tissue disruption
  - Better healing
  - Minimal scarring
- **Perpendicular incision**: cuts across bundles
  - Greater tissue damage
  - More scarring
  - Slower or poorer healing
01.05.03.05.017sa: In which direction relative to cleavage lines should a surgeon ideally orient an incision, and why?
Parallel, because it separates rather than cuts across collagen bundles, resulting in better healing and less scarring

## Surgical Incision Planning
- Ideal orientation: **parallel to cleavage lines**
- Cleavage lines reflect collagen bundle direction in the reticular dermis
- Parallel cuts separate bundles naturally, minimizing:
  - Tissue disruption
  - Scar formation
  - Healing time
- Cutting perpendicular to cleavage lines severs more collagen bundles, increasing scarring
01.05.03.05.018sa: What is the medical term for stretch marks?

## Stretch Marks (Striae)
- **Striae** is the medical term for stretch marks
- Caused by the dermis stretching beyond its elastic capacity
- Result: tearing of collagen and elastic fibers
- Color progression:
  1. Initially red/purple (from visible blood vessels through thinned tissue)
  2. Fade to silvery-white (scar tissue replacement)
- Common causes: rapid weight gain, pregnancy, adolescent growth spurts
01.05.03.05.019sa: What skin layer is damaged when stretch marks form?

## Stretch Mark Formation
- Stretch marks result from damage to the **dermis**
- Specifically, the dermis is stretched beyond its elastic capacity
- Collagen and elastic fibers tear
- The epidermis remains intact but the underlying dermal damage is visible through it
- Common triggers: rapid weight gain, pregnancy, adolescent growth spurts
01.05.03.05.020sa: What specific structural event in the dermis causes stretch marks to form?
Tearing of collagen and elastic fibers when stretched beyond elastic capacity

## Mechanism of Stretch Mark Formation
- The dermis has an **elastic capacity** determined by its collagen and elastic fiber content
- When stretched beyond this limit, both fiber types **tear**
- This tearing produces visible marks (striae)
- Color changes reflect the healing process:
  - Red/purple initially (exposed blood vessels)
  - Silvery-white over time (scar tissue)
01.05.03.05.021sa: Why do new stretch marks initially appear red or purple?
Blood vessels in the damaged dermis are visible through the thinned tissue

## Stretch Mark Color Progression
- **New striae**: red or purple
  - Dermal tearing exposes underlying blood vessels
  - Vessels are visible through the damaged, thinned tissue
- **Old striae**: silvery-white
  - Scar tissue replaces the torn fibers
  - Scar tissue is avascular, producing the pale appearance
01.05.03.05.022sa: What color do stretch marks fade to over time, and what type of tissue produces that appearance?

## Stretch Mark Maturation
- Over time, striae fade from red/purple to **silvery-white**
- The white color comes from **scar tissue** that replaces the torn collagen and elastic fibers
- Scar tissue lacks the vascularity of normal dermis
- Initial red/purple color was from visible blood vessels through damaged tissue
01.05.03.05.023sa: Name three common causes of stretch marks.
Rapid weight gain, pregnancy, and adolescent growth spurts

## Causes of Stretch Marks
- All three common causes involve rapid expansion of the skin:
  1. **Rapid weight gain**: fat accumulation stretches overlying skin
  2. **Pregnancy**: abdominal skin stretches as the uterus expands
  3. **Adolescent growth spurts**: rapid bone/muscle growth outpaces skin elasticity
- In each case, the dermis is stretched beyond its elastic capacity, tearing collagen and elastic fibers
01.05.03.05.024sa: What two fiber types are torn when the dermis is stretched beyond its elastic capacity?

## Dermal Fiber Tearing in Striae
- Both major dermal fiber types are damaged:
  1. **Collagen fibers**: provide tensile strength
  2. **Elastic fibers**: provide stretch and recoil
- Tearing of both produces visible stretch marks (striae)
- These fibers are replaced by scar tissue during healing, which is why mature striae appear silvery-white
01.05.03.05.025sa: What protein accounts for the dermis's resistance to stretching and tearing?

## Collagen and Tensile Strength
- **Collagen** (primarily type I) provides tensile strength
- Makes up ~70-80% of the dermis by dry weight
- Tensile strength = resistance to stretching and tearing
- Works alongside elastin, which provides recoil
- Collagen bundle orientation in the reticular dermis determines cleavage line patterns
01.05.03.05.026sa: What protein is responsible for the skin's ability to snap back after being stretched?

## Elastin and Skin Recoil
- **Elastin** is the protein responsible for stretch and recoil in the dermis
- Part of elastic fibers (elastin + fibrillin microfibrils)
- Works with collagen to allow young skin to stretch and snap back
- Age-related degradation of elastin reduces recoil, contributing to wrinkles and sagging
01.05.03.05.027sa: What structural change in the skin explains why aging skin wrinkles and sags rather than snapping back?
Degradation of both collagen and elastin

## Aging and Dermal Fiber Degradation
- Aging degrades both major structural fibers:
  - **Collagen**: loss of tensile strength
  - **Elastin**: loss of recoil
- Combined effect: skin can no longer stretch and snap back
- Visible results:
  - **Wrinkles**: loss of recoil leaves creases
  - **Sagging**: loss of structural support allows skin to droop
01.05.03.05.028sa: Cleavage lines represent the direction of greatest __________ in a given skin region.

## Cleavage Lines and Mechanical Stress
- Collagen bundles in the reticular dermis align along the direction of **greatest mechanical stress**
- This alignment defines the cleavage lines (tension lines / Langer lines)
- Orientation varies by region:
  - Limbs: longitudinal
  - Trunk/neck: circumferential
- Understanding this pattern is critical for surgical incision planning
01.05.03.05.029sa: What is the difference in cleavage line orientation between the limbs and the trunk?
Longitudinal on the limbs; circumferential on the trunk

## Regional Cleavage Line Patterns
- **Limbs**: longitudinal (running along the length)
- **Trunk and neck**: circumferential (wrapping around the body)
- These orientations reflect how collagen bundles are arranged in the reticular dermis of each region
- Each pattern aligns with the direction of greatest mechanical stress for that area
01.05.03.05.030sa: A surgical incision that cuts across collagen bundles rather than separating them will produce what undesirable outcome?

## Incision Orientation and Scarring
- Cutting **perpendicular** to cleavage lines severs collagen bundles
- This causes:
  - Greater tissue disruption
  - More scar formation
  - Slower, poorer healing
- Cutting **parallel** to cleavage lines separates bundles along their natural orientation
  - Minimal disruption
  - Better healing with less scarring
